title: jit-agents-spec.md is the outlier substrate text — describes phantom-tool-only dispatch contradicting canonical body (FEAT-005/006/DEC-0047/TASK-091/JI-029) which declares tool-using agents; spec doc is being amended to match canonical body
description: jit-agents-spec.md at docs/planning/jit-agents-spec.md describes the dispatch as phantom-tool-only — verbatim: 'executeAgent is unified in-process dispatch. Phantom tool enforcement via buildPhantomTool + forced toolChoice'; 'The same dispatch runtime sends it to the LLM with the same phantom-tool structured-output enforcement.' The implementation at packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts matches the spec doc exactly (FGAP-169: compiled.tools discarded at line 488; only phantom-tool delivered at line 568 when outputSchema set). The canonical body for jit-agents is FEAT-005 verbatim ('The subagent receives its OWN tools, JIT-composed per invocation, scoped to EXACTLY the operations the task needs'), FEAT-006 ('constrained-orchestrator → spec-block → privileged-agent → real-check-validated → attested-commit code-change loop'), DEC-0047 (capability-grant model: spec-declared, default-empty, operation-granular, clamped at dispatch — clamp requires that granted capabilities then EXIST as agent-accessible tools), TASK-091 ('agent does the work, real-check verifies, attested-commit lands'), JI-029 ('our tool for deciding valid + current is simply the work of a jit agent with perms for specific things — read, amend blocks'). The canonical body describes tool-using agents acting on granted capabilities. jit-agents-spec.md is the outlier substrate text relative to that canonical body. The spec doc is being amended to match.

evidence.reference: Body A canon: 'executeAgent is unified in-process dispatch. Phantom tool enforcement via buildPhantomTool + forced toolChoice. Thinking not passed (Anthropic API constraint documented).' Spec defines dispatch as phantom-tool-only; no multi-tool path declared.

Body A implementation: compiled.tools at line 488 (computeGrantViolation grant-clamp check, then discarded); context.tools = [phantomTool] at line 568 (only when outputSchema set). Single completeFn call at line 572. No multi-tool wiring.

Body B canon: FEAT-005 verbatim 'The subagent receives its OWN tools, JIT-composed per invocation, scoped to EXACTLY the operations the task needs'; FEAT-006 north-star 'constrained-orchestrator → spec-block → privileged-agent → real-check-validated → attested-commit code-change loop' — privileged agent is one that acts with privileges, not one that classifies.

Body B canon: DEC-0047 enacted 'capability model: spec-declared, default EMPTY, operation-granular, clamped ⊆ parent at dispatch; capability-widening writes are writer.kind=human; terminal verdict = real deterministic checks the executive cannot fake' — the clamp at dispatch logically requires that granted capabilities then exist as agent-accessible tools; line 488 implementation clamps then discards.

Body B canon: TASK-091 (completed 2026-05-28, run-work-order-loop landing) frames the loop as 'agent does the work, real-check verifies, attested-commit lands'; the loop's value-proposition depends on the agent being able to do the work.

Body B user-intent verbatim: 'our tool for deciding valid + current is simply the work of a jit agent with perms for specific things — read, amend blocks.' User-stated intent explicitly assigns read+write actions to jit agents.

Companion gap (sharpened in commit a02581f): documents the implementation-level fact that compiled.tools is structurally unreachable; this FGAP escalates from implementation-fact to substrate-coherence-issue.

impact: jit-agents-spec.md served as substrate authority for the executeAgent implementation, which built the classifier/transformer-pattern dispatch matching the spec. The rest of the substrate — capability-grant infrastructure, north-star framing, user-stated intentions, completed TASKs that depend on agent tool-use — continues to advertise tool-using-agent semantics. Authors reading the substrate find a framework that promises tool-using privileged agents and use the surface as if those promises hold; agents fail silently at dispatch. The capability-grant infrastructure built per FEAT-004/005 + DEC-0047 + TASK-089 (composeToolGrant, clamp semantics, author-tool-grant Pi tool, operation-granular grants) provides no value at agent dispatch — the grants are computed, clamped, then discarded with the tools. Every clock-menu-app agent spec declaring tools[] is broken-by-architecture as a consequence (per FGAP-169).
canonical_vocabulary: substrate coherence; canon-internal contradiction; substrate-implementation divergence
proposed_resolution: Substrate alignment: amend jit-agents-spec.md to describe multi-tool dispatch as canonical and the phantom-tool forced-toolChoice pattern as one mode-among-modes (classifier-mode for structured-output verdicts via outputSchema; agentic-mode for multi-turn tool-using work via declared tools[]). The amendment writes the canonical body's commitments (FEAT-005/006/DEC-0047/TASK-091/JI-029) into the spec doc that the implementation reads as its design source. Implementation work follows per FGAP-169 proposed_resolution (multi-tool dispatch loop in executeAgent).
